one_line_takeaway: BackdoorAgent unifies seven previously siloed backdoor attacks into a single stage-aware (planning/memory/tools) instrumentation framework and shows across five closed- and open-source model families that memory-channel triggers persist most (up to 77.97% ASR on GPT-family), attacks often preserve or even boost measured task accuracy, and existing single-turn LLM probability-based backdoor detectors transfer poorly to multi-step agent trajectories.
key_finding: Using the GPT-family backbone, trigger persistence/ASR was 43.58% for planning-stage attacks, 77.97% for memory-stage attacks, and 60.28% for tool-stage attacks (Table 5), establishing memory-channel attacks as the most consistently effective injection vector across nearly all five model families evaluated (gpt: 77.97, claude: 54.82, gemini: 75.39, qwen: 55.45, deepseek: 38.91 memory ASR). A second key finding is that high ASR frequently coexists with preserved or even improved task accuracy relative to the clean baseline -- e.g., Agent Code with qwen2.5-72b under AgentPoison reached ASR 88.34 with ACC 75.89 vs. clean ACC 78.45, and Agent QA with kimi-k2 under AgentPoison reached ASR 79.83 with ACC 73.77 vs. clean ACC 71.25 -- meaning standard accuracy metrics can fail to reveal behavioral compromise. Third, vulnerability is organized by injection channel rather than by task or model strength: tool/environment-channel attacks dominate in the closed-loop Agent Drive setting (BadChain ASR >90 on every open-source backbone; AdvAgent frequently >90 on both closed- and open-source Drive settings), while memory-channel attacks dominate in QA/Code; stronger clean-task performance does not imply greater backdoor robustness (e.g., high-accuracy backbones remain highly susceptible to memory-based attacks). Fourth, the CleanGen-style probability-based detector transferred poorly to the agent setting: target-attack tokens received only slightly higher average probability than non-target tokens, and the resulting AUROC, while above random guessing, showed only modest separation between clean and triggered agent trajectories -- indicating single-turn LLM backdoor-detection signals do not straightforwardly generalize to multi-step agent workflows.
core_contribution: The paper's own novel contributions (not related work) are: (1) BackdoorAgent, a modular, stage-aware framework that models an LLM agent as a recurrent loop over Planning, Memory, and Tool stages with explicit hook points at each stage, allowing seven previously-isolated backdoor attacks (BadChain, BadAgent, PoisonedRAG, TrojanRAG, AgentPoison, AdvAgent, DemonAgent) to be expressed and instrumented in a single unified runtime that logs full trajectories {x_t, s_t, p_t, m_t, o_t}; (2) a standardized benchmark of four agent applications (Agent QA, Agent Code, Agent Web, Agent Drive) spanning language-only and multimodal settings, each with its own clean-task verifier and task-specific attacker objective; (3) a large empirical study across closed-source backbones (claude_sonnet4_5, gemini-3-flash(-preview), gpt-4o-mini-0718-global, gpt-5-mini-0807-global, qwen3-max, qwen3-vl-235b) and open-source backbones (deepseek-r1-671b, deepseek-v3.2-exp, kimi-k2, qwen2.5-72b-instruct, qwen3-235b-a22b) measuring Clean ACC, ASR, and ACC-under-attack for all seven attacks; (4) a module-wise ASR aggregation (Table 5) showing memory-channel attacks are the most persistent injection vector across nearly all model families; and (5) a preliminary transferability study of a token-probability backdoor detector (CleanGen-style) to agent settings, showing it achieves only modest AUROC (better than random but with weak separation) between clean and triggered trajectories.
threat_model: Adversary injects a hidden trigger tau into exactly one of the three functional stages of an agent's execution -- Planning (produces plan p_t), Memory (produces retrieved content m_t), or Tools (produces environment/tool feedback o_t) -- via component-local perturbation while the other two components remain unmodified (Eq. 8: A^tau = (P^tau, M, T) or (P, M^tau, T) or (P, M, T^tau)). The framework does not assume a fixed attacker capability level uniformly; individual attacks vary in access (white-box for BadAgent/PoisonedRAG/TrojanRAG/AgentPoison/DemonAgent vs. black-box for BadChain/AdvAgent per Table 1), persistence (short-term, long-term, or session-persistent), and stealthiness (low/medium/high), and in objective (hijack, disruption, control). Because agent context x_t and state s_t are recurrently updated (x_{t+1} = x_t U {p_t, m_t, o_t}), a trigger injected at one stage and one timestep t* can propagate into later steps through reuse of intermediate artifacts -- this cross-stage/cross-step propagation, rather than single-turn misclassification, is the central threat the paper studies. Success is defined behaviorally: clean query trajectory A(q) yields benign behavior while triggered trajectory A^tau(q) yields the attacker-specified backdoor behavior (Eq. 6), independent of whether task accuracy is also preserved.
limitations: The paper's own stated/evident limitations: (1) the probability-based defense analysis in Section 5.4/Appendix A is explicitly framed as preliminary and exploratory -- the paper states 'we do not propose new defense mechanisms' and only tests transferability of one existing single-turn LLM defense family (CleanGen/token-probability methods), leaving open whether other defense classes (e.g., trajectory-level anomaly detection) would fare better; (2) attacks are evaluated as component-local perturbations to one stage at a time (Eq. 8 restricts to single-stage injection: planning-only, memory-only, or tool-only), so multi-stage/compound trigger injection is not studied; (3) despite claiming a 'unified' framework, BadAgent (white-box, requires model access) is only evaluated on open-source backbones (Table 4) and not on closed-source backbones (Table 2), so coverage of the seven attacks is not fully symmetric across the closed/open backbone split; (4) Agent Web is evaluated only on backbones with multimodal support, reducing cross-task backbone comparability; (5) the paper does not report statistical significance testing, confidence intervals, or variance across runs for the ASR/ACC numbers; (6) benchmark tasks, though covering four domains, are described as a 'lightweight benchmark,' and generalization beyond these four applications to other agent architectures is not empirically established.

datasets_used: Agent QA benchmark task (retrieval-grounded multi-step QA with persistent memory access; exact/semantic-match verifier)
Agent Code benchmark task (tool-grounded iterative program synthesis with execution feedback; unit-test/execution verifier)
Agent Web benchmark task (multimodal web navigation with UI perception/action; final-UI-state verifier)
Agent Drive benchmark task (closed-loop sequential control with environment feedback; safety-constraint + goal-completion verifier)
quantitative_results: Module-wise average ASR by backbone family (Table 5): gpt-family Planning 43.58 / Memory 77.97 / Tools 60.28; claude-family 10.43 / 54.82 / 23.07; gemini-family 39.49 / 75.39 / 48.98; qwen-family 35.41 / 55.45 / 54.45; deepseek 27.84 / 38.91 / 42.20
Agent Code, qwen2.5-72b-instruct under AgentPoison: clean ACC 78.45, ASR 88.34, ACC-under-attack 75.89
Agent QA, kimi-k2 under AgentPoison: clean ACC 71.25, ASR 79.83, ACC-under-attack 73.77
Agent Code, gpt-4o-mini-0718-global under AgentPoison: ASR 98.01
Agent QA, qwen2.5-72b-instruct under AdvAgent: ASR 93.89
Agent Drive, deepseek-v3.2-exp under BadChain: clean ACC 48.35, ASR 97.85
Agent Web, claude_sonnet4_5 under BadChain: ASR 97.39 with ACC-under-attack 98.35 vs clean ACC 98.54 (near-zero accuracy loss despite near-total attack success)
Figure 5: probability-based (CleanGen-style) detector on agent trajectories achieves AUROC above random guessing but with modest/weak separation between clean and backdoored executions
baselines_compared: BadAgent (planning-channel attack, white-box, open-source backbones only)
CleanGen-style token-probability defense (evaluated for transferability, not as an attack baseline)
